summary: I went to Mississippi in 1963 to work on a literacy project with Bob Moses and the SNCC group. It was a dangerous place for civil rights work, but I felt privileged to be part of the movement there. We were building parallel institutions like the Freedom Vote and Freedom Schools because segregation excluded Black people from regular institutions. Living and working in places like Tougaloo and Jackson, Mississippi, I experienced firsthand the violence and hostility but also the resilience of the community.

Bob Moses was a visionary leader who empowered people rather than leading from the front. He had a unique ability to help people connect with their true feelings and aspirations, creating a kindred spirit among us. I worked closely with him in Mississippi starting in 1963 on a literacy project funded by a grant he secured. His leadership was comforting and inspiring, and he was central to the work we did with SNCC and Freedom Summer.

I was part of the SNCC group that went to Mississippi in 1963, and I was the first white woman to join them there. SNCC was deeply involved in creating parallel institutions like the Freedom Vote and Freedom Schools to counter segregation. I worked in the SNCC office in Atlanta before moving into the field in Mississippi, coordinating efforts and supporting volunteers during Freedom Summer. SNCC was at the heart of the civil rights movement in the South, especially in places like Mississippi and Georgia.

Freedom Summer in 1964 was a major campaign to break open the rigid caste system of segregation in Mississippi. We brought in northern college students to support voter registration and build community institutions, despite the constant threat of violence. It was a blitzkrieg effort to punch a hole in the system, and I was coordinating staff and supporting volunteers, many of whom experienced culture shock. Freedom Summer was about creating real human interactions between Black and white people and gaining outside support to challenge segregation.

The Freedom Vote was a parallel voting campaign we organized in Mississippi in 1963 to challenge the exclusion of Black voters under segregation. Since Black people were barred from official voting, we created a separate ballot to demonstrate the desire and readiness of Black citizens to participate in democracy. This was part of a broader strategy of building parallel institutions, like the Freedom Schools, to empower the Black community despite systemic barriers.

Freedom Schools were part of the strategy to create parallel institutions in Mississippi during the civil rights movement. These schools provided education and empowerment to Black children and adults who were excluded from quality schooling due to segregation. We developed them after the Freedom Vote as a way to build community strength and consciousness. They were a vital part of the movement’s effort to break down the barriers imposed by segregation.

I remember going to Greenwood, Mississippi, during the summer of 1964 with a reporter, and it was a tense experience. We were even run off the road by hostile locals, which was frightening. Greenwood was one of the places where the civil rights struggle was intense, and the dangers were very real. It was part of the broader Mississippi campaign where we lived and worked, often facing threats but also finding strength in the community.

I lived at Tougaloo College outside Jackson, Mississippi, which served as a sort of safe haven and R & R center during Freedom Summer. It was an all-Black community where many activists could rest, eat, and regroup amidst the constant pressure and danger. Tougaloo was a hub for organizing and a refuge for those working in the movement, and I spent a lot of time there coordinating staff and supporting volunteers.

Jackson, Mississippi, was a central location for our civil rights work, and I often traveled between Tougaloo and Jackson. The city was a place of tension, where local police sometimes detained me because they couldn’t understand why a white woman was involved in the movement. Jackson was also where the SNCC office was located, and it was a key site for coordinating voter registration and Freedom Summer activities.

Before coming to Mississippi, I had experience working in Albany, Georgia, where the civil rights movement was also active. Volunteers there often experienced culture shock due to poverty, heat, and the disorganized style of the Black community. The reception from the Black community was mixed, with both hostility and warmth, which affected the volunteers deeply. Albany was a place where many of the lessons about fear, fatigue, and community dynamics were learned before the larger Freedom Summer campaign.

In the year before Freedom Summer, I worked in southwest Georgia with some of the new white volunteers from the North. It was there that many of them first encountered the realities of living and working in a Black community under segregation. That experience prepared us somewhat for the larger and more intense campaign in Mississippi. Southwest Georgia was an important training ground for understanding the challenges and fears involved in the movement.
